title: Skills Investment Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
plain_summary: This bill changes special savings accounts that were only for school into accounts that can be used for learning new job skills throughout your whole life. People can put money into these accounts until they turn 70 years old and get tax breaks for doing so. Companies can also put money into their workers' accounts and get tax benefits too. The money in these accounts can pay for job training programs and classes that help people learn new skills for work.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Skills Investment Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Coverdell Lifelong Learning Accounts
sections.text: This section changes the tax code to expand education savings accounts into lifelong learning accounts. First, it renames all Coverdell education savings accounts to Coverdell lifelong learning accounts throughout the tax code. Any account that was set up before January first, two thousand twenty four, as an education savings account will automatically become a lifelong learning account. The section then expands what these accounts can pay for. In addition to regular school expenses, the accounts can now pay for job training and skill development programs after the person turns sixteen years old. These programs include workforce training services, career and technical education, career counseling services, youth job programs, and adult education classes. The accounts can also pay for transportation to these programs, testing fees needed for enrollment or certification, and computer equipment or software needed for the training. The section changes the age rules for these accounts. People can now make contributions until age seventy instead of age eighteen. After age thirty, the account balance cannot exceed ten thousand dollars, and contributions are limited to four thousand dollars per year instead of two thousand dollars. People over thirty cannot change who benefits from their account. The section creates a tax credit for employers who contribute to their employees' lifelong learning accounts. Employers can get a credit equal to twenty five percent of what they contribute. The section also lets people who are eighteen or older take a tax deduction for money they put into their own lifelong learning accounts. However, if they take money out later, they will have to pay income tax on the amount they deducted, plus a twenty percent penalty instead of ten percent. These changes take effect on January first, two thousand twenty six.
